result:
There’s a moment in late summer when the air begins to remember the coming cold. It lingers on the skin like the edge of a thought you didn’t quite catch—warm where the sun lands, cool in every shadow. A red-winged blackbird drops from the power line, wings flicking half open, then folds back into himself like a book snapping shut. Wind rattles what’s left of the cottonwood leaves, and everything smells faintly of sweet, baked dust.

Inside my head is a different season. There, snow is already falling in the dark behind my eyelids—white, intent flakes that whisper, driftless, until they gutter out against an unseen windowpane. Two weather systems are always operating in parallel: the one outside my skin and the one inside. There should be a word for how they send each other postcards.

Sometimes I carry around a pebble in my pocket for weeks. One I almost on a riverbank, flattened and slate-gray. I promise myself I’ll throw it into the next moving water, one river trading it for another, but I never do. That pebble is weightless and heavy at the same time—absolutely useless, yet essential. An abstract noun disguised as geology. Maybe it’s the physical proof that I can hold on to nothing and still have something.

At night the freeway beyond the cedars thrums like a distant mind sorting its memories. Headlights crest the hill, pan across the ceiling, and slide away. Each brief glare is a short chapter I can’t quite read. Stories entire and discarded in the space of a breath: someone driving home from a gig that ended too early, someone leaving a hospital with better news than expected, somebody simply low on gas.

The day is littered with those almost-stories. A barista whose eyeliner is half-washed away, an old man gripping a newspaper with the delicate concentration of prayer, a child wearing one rain boot on a cloudless afternoon. Thousands of unopened envelopes, the whole world folded small and humming.

If you pressed your ear against the moment itself, like a conch shell, you would hear oceans nobody has bothered to name.

prompt: Write freely about whatever you want.
